title: Young's Modulus of Rock
aim: To determine the Young's Modulus of the given rock specimen.
theory: Young's Modulus is a measure of the stiffness or rigidity of a material.
It is defined as the ratio of stress to strain within the elastic limit of the material.
For rocks, it indicates how much the rock deforms under applied load.
The test is usually performed under uniaxial compression.
Stress is calculated as load divided by cross-sectional area.
Strain is calculated as change in length divided by original length.
Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.
The slope of the stress-strain curve in the elastic region gives Young's Modulus.
Higher value of Young's Modulus indicates stiffer rock.
Lower value indicates more deformable rock.
It is important in rock mechanics, tunneling, and foundation design.
It helps in understanding deformation behavior of rocks under load.
procedure: The rock specimen is prepared in cylindrical shape with smooth and parallel ends.
The dimensions (diameter and length) of the specimen are measured accurately.
The specimen is placed in the Compression Testing Machine (CTM).
An extensometer or dial gauge is attached to measure deformation.
Load is applied gradually and uniformly without shock.
At regular intervals, corresponding deformation readings are recorded.
Loading is continued within the elastic limit of the specimen.
Stress and strain values are calculated for each reading.
The slope of the linear portion of the graph is determined.
Young's Modulus is calculated using the slope.
The experiment is repeated and average value is reported.
result: The Young's Modulus of the given rock sample is found to be 50 GPa.
